title: 001 — A Radial Decision Boundary for a Toy Classification Model
text:
← Back to derivations
31 May 2026
A placeholder file for derivations and notes on the radial-boundary toy classification problem.

title: 001 — A Radial Decision Boundary for a Toy Classification Model
text:
Questions

What is the probability that a random point \(X=(X_1,X_2)\), distributed according to a distribution \(P_X\) centered at the origin, lies within the Euclidean ball \[ \mathcal B_2(0,r) = \{x\in\mathbb R^2:\|x\|_2\le r\} \] in the following cases?


\(P_X\) is the uniform distribution on the square \([-a,a]^2\), with independent coordinates, for some \(a>r\ge 0\).
\(P_X\) is the bivariate normal distribution \(\mathcal N(0,\sigma^2 I_2)\), for some \(\sigma>0\).


Consider the following binary classification model. Let \(f(X)=\mathbf 1_{\{X\in \mathcal B_2(0,r)\}}.\) The observed label \(Y\) is defined by \[ Y = \begin{cases}
   f(X), & \text{with probability } 1-\eta,\\
   1-f(X), & \text{with probability } \eta.
   \end{cases} \]


What is the probability that \(Y=1\) in this model for the cases considered in Question 1?

title: 001 — A Radial Decision Boundary for a Toy Classification Model
text:
Derivation

1. Probability of the ball event
The following two cases assume the noiseless setting where the label is the indicator of membership in the ball: \(Y = \mathbf{1}_{X \in \mathcal B_2(0,r)}\).

Uniform distribution on ([-a,a]^2).

Since \(X_1\) and \(X_2\) are independent and uniformly distributed on \([-a,a]\), the joint density function of \(X\) is given by \[f_X(x_1,x_2) = \frac{1}{4a^2} \quad \text{for } (x_1,x_2) \in [-a,a]^2\] and \(f_X(x_1,x_2) = 0\) otherwise.The probability that \(X\) lies within the ball \(\mathcal B_2(0,r)\) is given by the integral of the density function over the region defined by the ball:
\[\begin{align*}
\mathbb{P}(Y=1) &= \int_{\mathcal B_2(0,r)} f_X(x_1,x_2) , dx_1 dx_2 \\\
&= \int_{x_1=-a}^{a} \int_{x_2=-a}^{a} \frac{1}{4a^2} \mathbf{1}_{x_1^2 + x_2^2 \le r^2} , dx_1 dx_2 \\\
&= \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^r \frac{1}{4a^2} \rho , d\rho d\theta \\\
&= \frac{\pi r^2}{4a^2}.
\end{align*}\]
Geometric interpretation: The probability \(\mathbb{P}(Y=1)\) can be interpreted as the ratio of the area of the circle defined by the ball \(\mathcal B_2(0,r)\) to the area of the square \([-a,a]^2\). The area of the circle is \(\pi r^2\) and the area of the square is \(4a^2\), hence the probability is \(\frac{\pi r^2}{4a^2}\). More precisely,
\[\begin{align*}\mathbb{P}(Y=1) &= \frac{\lambda(\mathcal B_2(0,r) \cap [-a,a]^2)}{\lambda([-a,a]^2)} \\\
&= \frac{\pi r^2}{4a^2},\end{align*}\] where \(\lambda\) denotes the Lebesgue measure on \(\mathbb{R}^2\).

Bivariate normal distribution \(\mathcal N(0,\sigma^2 I_2)\).

Let \(X \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 I_2)\). The probability that \(X\) lies within the ball \(\mathcal B_2(0,r)\) is given by \[\mathbb{P}(Y=1) = \mathbb{P}(\|X\|_2 \le r) = \mathbb{P}\left(\frac{\|X\|_2^2}{\sigma^2} \le \frac{r^2}{\sigma^2}\right).\]
Since \(\frac{\|X\|_2^2}{\sigma^2} = \frac{X_1^2 + X_2^2}{\sigma^2}\) follows a chi-squared distribution with 2 degrees of freedom, we have
\[\mathbb{P}(Y=1) = F_{\chi^2_2}\left(\frac{r^2}{\sigma^2}\right),\]
where \(F_{\chi^2_2}\) is the cumulative distribution function of the chi-squared distribution with 2 degrees of freedom.
The other calculation approach is the same as in case 1, but with the density function of the bivariate normal distribution: \[f_X(x_1,x_2) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} \exp\left(-\frac{x_1^2 + x_2^2}{2\sigma^2}\right).\] The probability can be computed as \[\begin{align*}\mathbb{P}(Y=1) &= \int_{\mathcal B_2(0,r)} f_X(x_1,x_2) , dx_1 dx_2 \\\
&= \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^r \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} \exp\left(-\frac{\rho^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \rho , d\rho d\theta \\\
&= 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{r^2}{2\sigma^2}\right).\end{align*}\]


2. Noisy model — label noise \(\eta\)
The following assumes the noisy label model described in Question 2: with probability \(1-\eta\) the label equals \(f(X)=\mathbf{1}_{X\in\mathcal B_2(0,r)}\), and with probability \(\eta\) it is flipped.

Recall the noiseless (true) label is \(Y_0=1\) when \(X\in\mathcal B_2(0,r)\) and \(Y_0=0\) otherwise. In the noisy case, the observed label equals the true label with probability \((1-\eta)\) and is flipped with probability \(\eta\). Hence \[p(x) := \mathbb{P}(Y=1 \mid X=x) = (1-\eta)f(x) + \eta (1-f(x)).\]
Integrating over \(X\) gives

\[\begin{aligned}\mathbb{P}(Y=1) &= \int_{\mathbb{R}^2} \mathbb{P}(Y=1 \mid X=x) f_X(x) , dx \\\
&= (1-\eta)\int f(x)f_X(x) ,dx + \eta\int (1-f(x))f_X(x) ,dx.
\end{aligned}\]
Let \(q=\mathbb{P}(X\in\mathcal B_2(0,r))\). Then the above simplifies to \[\mathbb{P}(Y=1) = (1-\eta) q + \eta (1-q) = \eta + (1-2\eta)q.\]
For the uniform case \(q=\pi r^2/(4a^2)\), so
\[\mathbb{P}(Y=1) = \eta + (1-2\eta)\frac{\pi r^2}{4a^2}.\]
For the Gaussian case \(q=1-\exp(-r^2/(2\sigma^2))\), so \[\mathbb{P}(Y=1) = \eta + (1-2\eta)\left(1- e^{-r^2/(2\sigma^2)}\right).\]

title: 002 — Deriving the Bayes Classifier under 0–1 Loss
text:
← Back to derivations
03 June 2026
A placeholder file for derivations and notes on the Risk functional in the binary classification setting.

title: 002 — Deriving the Bayes Classifier under 0–1 Loss
text:
Question
Given a binary classification problem with \(Y \in \{0,1\}\), derive the Bayes classifier under the \(0\)-\(1\) loss and calculate the corresponding Bayes risk.

title: 002 — Deriving the Bayes Classifier under 0–1 Loss
text:
Setup
We recall the following definitions:

The \(0\)-\(1\) loss is defined as \[
L(y, \hat{y}) = \mathbf{1}_{\{y \neq \hat{y}\}} =
\begin{cases}
0, & \text{if } y = \hat{y}, \\
1, & \text{if } y \neq \hat{y}.
\end{cases}
\]
The risk of a classifier \(g:\mathbb{R}^d \to \{0,1\}\) under \(0\)-\(1\) loss is \[
R(g) = \mathbb{E}[L(Y, g(X))] = \mathbb{P}(Y \neq g(X)).
\]

We also recall that the Bayes optimal classifier \(g^*\) is the classifier that minimises the risk over all possible classifiers. The corresponding minimum risk is called the Bayes risk \(R^*\). More formally, \[g^* = \arg\min_{g} R(g), \quad R^* = R(g^*).\]
We also recall the following lemma which provides an alternative approach to find a Bayes optimal classifier:






NoteLemma (Bayes optimal classifier)



If \(g^\ast\) is a classifier that minimises the conditional risk \[
R^{cond}(g \mid X=x)= \int L(y, g(x)) dP_{Y|X=x}(y)
\] for almost every \(x\), then \(g^\ast\) is a Bayes optimal classifier.








TipProof



Let \(g\) be any classifier. Then we can write the risk of \(g\) as \[\begin{aligned}
R(g) &= \mathbb{E}[L(Y, g(X))] = \mathbb{E}[L(Y, g(X))X] \\\
&= \int \int L(y, g(x)) dP_{Y|X=x}(y) dP_X(x) \\\
&= \int R^{cond}(g \mid X=x) dP_X(x).
\end{aligned}\] Since \(g^\ast\) minimises the conditional risk for almost every \(x\), we have \[R^{cond}(g^\ast \mid X=x) \le R^{cond}(g \mid X=x)\] for almost every \(x\). Integrating both sides with respect to \(P_X\) gives \[R(g^\ast) = \int R^{cond}(g^\ast \mid X=x) dP_X(x) \le \int R^{cond}(g \mid X=x) dP_X(x) = R(g).\] Since \(g\) was arbitrary, we conclude that \(g^\ast\) is a Bayes optimal classifier.


The above lemma states that to find a Bayes optimal classifier, it suffices to find a classifier that minimises the conditional risk for each fixed \(x\). This is often easier than directly minimising the overall risk, as the minimisation problem involves only the conditional distribution of \(Y\) given \(X=x\) rather than the joint distribution of \((X,Y)\).

title: 002 — Deriving the Bayes Classifier under 0–1 Loss
text:
Derivation

Let \(g:\mathbb{R}^d \to \{0,1\}\) be any classifier. The conditional risk of \(g\) under \(0\)-\(1\) loss can be expressed as

\[\begin{aligned}
R^{cond}(g \mid X=x) &= \mathbb{P}(Y \neq g(X) \mid X=x) \\\
&= \mathbb{P}(Y=1 \mid X=x) \mathbf{1}_{g(x)=0} + \mathbb{P}(Y=0 \mid X=x) \mathbf{1}_{g(x)=1} \\\
&= \eta(x) (1-g(x)) + (1-\eta(x)) g(x), \\\
\end{aligned}\] where \(\eta(x) := \mathbb{P}(Y=1 \mid X=x)\) is the regression function.

To find the Bayes optimal classifier, it suffices to minimise the conditional risk \(R^{cond}(g \mid X=x)\) with respect to \(g(x)\) for each fixed \(x\). Since \(g(x)\) can only take values in \(\{0,1\}\), we can evaluate the conditional risk for both cases:


If \(g(x)=0\), then \[R^{cond}(g \mid X=x) = \eta(x).\]
If \(g(x)=1\), then \[R^{cond}(g \mid X=x) = 1-\eta(x).\]


The conditional risk is minimal if we choose \(g(x)=1\) when \(\eta(x) > 1-\eta(x)\), and \(g(x)=0\) otherwise. This can be rewritten as \[g^*(x) = \mathbf{1}_{\{\eta(x) > 1/2\}}.\]
By the above lemma, \(g^*\) is a Bayes optimal classifier. Hence, the Bayes optimal classifier under \(0\)-\(1\) loss is given by \[g^*(x) = \mathbf{1}_{\{\eta(x) > 1/2\}}.\]
The Bayes risk can be calculated as \[\begin{aligned}
R^* &= R(g^*) = \mathbb{E}[L(Y, g^*(X))] = \mathbb{P}(Y \neq g^*(X)) \\\
&= \mathbb{E}[\min\{\eta(X), 1-\eta(X)\}].
\end{aligned}\]

title: 002 — Deriving the Bayes Classifier under 0–1 Loss
text:
Remarks:


In the context of statistical learning theory, the variable \(X\) is often referred to as the feature or input, while the variable \(Y\) is referred to as the label or output. The regression function \(\eta(x)\) can be interpreted as the probability that the label is \(1\) given the feature \(x\). The conditional random variable \(Y\mid X\) is distributed according to a Bernoulli distribution with parameter \(\eta(X)\), which means that \(Y\) takes the value 1 with probability \(\eta(X)\) and the value 0 with probability \(1-\eta(X)\). None of the distributions of \(X\) or \(Y\mid X\) are assumed to be known in practice.
Intuitively, the Bayes classifier knows the regression function \(\eta\). It is the classifier we would use if we had access to the true distribution of \(Y\mid X\).
In practice, we cannot predict \(Y\) accurately. The classification error associated with a chosen classifier is not zero. The Bayes risk provides a lower bound on the classification error that can be achieved by any classifier. It is the best possible error rate that can be achieved given the distribution of the data and the noise in the labels.
While optimal, the Bayes classifier presents a major drawback: we cannot compute it because we do not know the regression function \(\eta(x)\). One of the main goals of statistical learning is to estimate \(\eta(x)\) from data, and then use the estimated regression function to construct a classifier that approximates the Bayes optimal classifier. For instance, one can assume a parametric model for \(\eta(x)\), such as logistic regression.
